segments.text: Have a good for time, but it'd be great.
But leave time for of questions if you can.
Yeah. Thank you. Um, I'm Claudia Inger.
I'm one of the commissioners on the
New Zealand Law Commission.
Um, we thought given the level of overlap
between the chisel judgment
and the issues that we canvas in our report,
that it was appropriate for us to come
and offer what assistance we can to the committee.
Our formal role ends when we submit a report,
and we do not advocate for implementation of our work.
So I'm not here to advocate, I'm just simply here
to provide what assistance I can.
In fact, when I say bye, I mean him
because I'm gonna hand over to my colleague Thomas Burt,
who's going to present the submission and, um, take the lead
and answer any questions.
Cool.
Good afternoon. Um, from myself.
Um, I'll try
and be as helpful as I can to the committee by explaining
how our recent report on preventive detention, extended
supervision orders and public protection orders relates
to the findings the Supreme Court made in the Chisel case.
Um, I'll keep my statement brief
and take our written submission as read,
if that's agreeable to you.
Yep. That's, and I'll just provide a little bit of context
of our review and then we can jump
to questions you may have.
Cool. Um, we commenced our review of the Law
of Preventive Detention, esos and PPOs
after the Court of Appeal had issued its, uh, judgment, um,
Um, we then, uh, undertook two rounds
of public consultation.
First on, um,
the issues we had had identified with the current law.
Second, with our draft recommendations to address them.
And throughout the process, we engaged with stakeholders,
experts, academics.
We submitted our final report to the Ministers of Justice
and Corrections in March last year.
And, um, that was, uh, four months
after the Supreme Court released its decision in Chisel.
Um, that was an intentional decision to wait for
that decision to come out so we could take it into account
for our final recommendations.
We, as we said in our, um, written submissions are confident
that, um, implementing our recommendations would both, uh,
meet both address the, uh,
human rights inconsistencies the Supreme Court has, um,
found and improve public safety.
I'll just say a little bit about what's in the final report.
Uh, the main issues we identified are, um,
the ongoing human rights challenges.
Uh, it with regarding to the sentence
of preventive detention, uh,
before the UN Human Rights Committee, and regarding esos
and PPOs before the domestic courts.
In addition to those human rights challenges,
we identified substantial fragmentation issues in
how the three relevant statutes, the Sentencing Act,
the Parole Act, and the Public Safety,
public Protection Orders Act overlap and work together.
There were several inconsistencies
because of the piecemeal way,
how those three statutes had developed over time.
The key recommendation we made in the report was
to repeal the current law, replace it with a new act,
and that that new act should only provide
for post sentence orders,
not a sentence like preventive detention.
We recommend carrying over several aspects
of the existing ESO and PPO regime into the new act,
but to improve it in some key areas.
That would, um, be the context I'd provide to you for now,
and I'm happy to answer any questions you may have.
Okay. We've got lots of lawyers in the room, um, right.
Who wants to kick off?
We go. No, I'm, I'm happy with that. Okay.
Oh yeah. Go. Um, Ms. Walter,
Um, thank you.
And thank you for the, uh, the table at the end
of your submission, which I found really helpful comparing
the chisel recommendations with your as well.
Um, one of the decision points in Sal was the reflection on,
um, retrospective application.
And I didn't see that unless I missed it in the table.
Did you have a recommendation on that
and whether there was a way,
'cause the decision was fairly definitive on that front.
Um, and then I've just got a second question as well.
Shall I answer that one first? Yeah.
Um, we did think quite a lot about
retrospective application.
Um, the details are in chapter 19 of our report,
we recommended that other Department
of Corrections is best place, best place
to make the call on transitional provisions
as it has significant resourcing implications.
What we did in our report is step through each scenario,
um, of which person is subject to which order,
whether it was, um, imposed, retrospectively,
or pro prospectively to begin with.
And according to the Supreme Court judgment,
what can be done in terms of transitional arrangements?
The Supreme Court in Chisel did, um,
exhaustively say that
any preventive measure involving detention
that is imposed retrospectively cannot be justified,
which limits the options.
Okay. So, so am I correct
that you are saying it potentially could be a case by case,
though, in terms of assessing the particular order,
the particular conditions,
but also potentially the particular risk in that case, the,
for the transition range for the transitions? Yeah,
That's right.
Um, and the second question was, um,
you made the point about it being post sentence.
You would've seen the Law Society's proposal that you,
one option was to do it as a conditional
provision attached to sentencing.
Um, is, could, could you just speak to the rationale
behind excluding that as an option?
Happy, happy to do that? Yes.
Um, before reaching our conclusion that, um,
our recommendation was going
to be an only post sentence order regime, we did step
through all alternatives we could conceive of, um,
including the one that, um, law Society, um,
brought up in their submission.
Our key reason for, um,
recommending a post sentence regime is that alternatives
that, um, add a mechanism to sentencing
do not necessarily fully address the issue
of a second punishment.
There's still a possibility
that the courts would find the subsequent imposition
of a measure to be a second punishment
despite it originating at the point of sentencing.
Right. The second issue we
identified with that approach is, um, if imposing
that measure, its sentencing without yet being able
to take into account whether the person,
the relevant person would go
through a rehabilitative journey in prison
or the opposite, um, be, um, uh, show signs
of a higher re-offending risk, it would not be possible
to take those conditions into account
at the point of sentencing.
Um, having a preliminary measure imposed
that can be later invalidated also risks making the path
seem like a fatal contemplate that is geared towards, um,
that person later on having that measure imposed.
Those were our main reason reasons for, um,
preferring the post sentence regime, uh, related
and secondary reason is
having only one point in time
where measures can be imposed mm-hmm.
Is the best setting for the court to be able to
assess all available measures in, uh, the full spectrum
of restrictiveness in one point in time without having
to make speculations
what order may be imposed at a later point in time,
how the person's risk will develop over time.
Uh, and I think that makes sense. Just outta curiosity.
Does, does any other jurisdiction do this in a way that has
that sentencing conditional measure imposed,
or is the most common way of approaching it post sentencing?
There is a range of approaches overseas,
and it's actually quite difficult
to discern a majority pathway or a,
or a best practice example.
Um, England and Wales, Canada
and, uh, Scotland, I believe all have, um, imposition
of preventive measures at sentencing.
Most Australian jurisdictions do it post sentence order,
but also at sentencing, we found that none
of those were convincing examples to lead us, uh,
to develop a best practice example.
They all, um, come with their own issues
and human rights litigation.
Okay. Cool. Any other questions from any members?
Um, just let's not back. Thank you, Mr. Chair.
Um, can we just get down to the basics here?
So, we are dealing with situations where we've got victims
of very serious offending.
So we wanna make sure these victims are protected from the
real high risk of re-offending.
And all I'm hearing about is the human rights
of these people that have enacted these disgusting,
horrible crimes against victims.
And I get it, human rights is something that should be
protected, but I keep coming back to the victims
and what, what we're trying to be convinced
that we should change in this matter
is extra protection for victims.
Mm-hmm. What do you have to say to that?
Please help me through that.
Um, I'll just, um, talk
to the reasoning we adopted in, in our report.
Yep. And that was that the important balancing act,
uh, to achieve in this area is between public safety
of the community on the one hand,
and the liberty rights of those subject
to preventive measures.
It is, um, uh, chapter three of our report deals
with the very first question, is there a need
to have something like preventive measures in place
to protect the community?
And our conclusion was, yes, that is necessary.
Most other, um, all other comparative jurisdictions
that we looked at have some form of measure.
And, um, were, were not for that measure.
That'd be, um, a risk of a very small group
of highly dangerous, um, offenders
to re-offend once they're released from a,
from a sentence of imprisonment.
As I said before, this public safety objective needs to be
balanced, um, with the human rights
of the individual person subject to a measure.
And what we found in developing our recommendations is
that usually the, the measures
that best achieve human rights consistency are also the best
measures to achieve public safety long term.
What I mean by that is, um, emphasizing the need
for rehabilitative programs
and, um, uh, a careful consideration of how a person,
uh, can attend programs
and make progress in their rehabilitative journey is
also a public safety.
Can I just add a little to that?
Just, um, in case it's not clear under the scheme
that we proposed all the same, like, types
of disposition would still be available all the way
through from a community based order
to detention in a secure facility to detention in prison if,
if people could not be managed in a secure facility.
And one of the things we were trying to do was to ensure
that all the measures that we know
that corrections find useful to have,
are on a very secure legislative footing.
Um, so that's another sort of thing underlying the report.
And as Thomas said, we were satisfied that by providing for
that secure legislative footing
and for by increasing opportunities for rehabilitation,
we would be improving public safety outcomes.
Okay. Let's store close here
because we've got three more submitters.
Um, I would suggest some members,
let's keep that theme going.
Um, it's very useful,
but thank you for making the opportunity
and actually turning up right now we've got Mr.
Mc, uh, Mr. Uh, Knight here. We've seen you before.
Mr. Knight. Yes. Nice to have you back.
We've got roughly 10 minutes ago. Thank you,
Committee. Um,
as you probably know, I'm a professor of Laura,
aka Victoria University with specialism within, uh,
constitutional law and government law.
And I am grateful for the opportunity to address you.
But, um, my submission's a little different
because I won't talk about PPOs or esos
'cause I have no particular claim to expertise on
that aspect of criminal justice.
But I would rather invite you
to reflect on the overarching issue of how you
as a parliamentary committee should respond to the advice
provided by the courts through a declaration as a matter
of general principle, political conscience or morality.
And my aim is, is hopefully that we might be able
to engender some form
of conventional expectation about parliament's posture
to the special process of constitutional dialogue, which is,
um, enlivened by, uh, these types of declarations.
And here, I, in my submission, I pointed to a convention
of the peers to be have developed in the uk,
which might be valuable,
but I think is one
that probably doesn't quite fit our constitutional setting.
So my specific recommendation is that the committee, uh,
should think about articulating some general principles,
speaking to how it might respond within the current
constitutional framework covered in standing order
processes, but the rights act
and the principle parliamentary sovereignty
more than simply doubling down on an impugned piece
of legislation, but less than blindly
or always adopting the court's advice
as a matter of formality.
There's a middle ground to be chartered here,
and I wanna recognize that that's something
that might require a bit of work
and conversation beyond the bounds of this, uh,
present matter before you in relation to PPOs and the sos.
And I'm suggesting you might consider appointing a
specialist advisor on the issue,
or even commencing a separate inquiry on that general point.
'cause I recognize that conversation's a, a tricky one,
but an important one because it's the meta
conversation that's in play here.
So I, if I illustrate this by, um, uh, sketching some
of the principles that I've suggested and, and they're
provided only on a very tentative, uh, sketch
because the things I'm still reflecting on
as this new remedy is finding its feet.
And my suggestion is the principle should be procedural
or perhaps better describes deliberative only,
not substantially saying
that you should necessarily follow the,
the court's advice in these circumstances,
but thinking about how you go about thinking
and grappling with it.
And, and those principles should be consistent
with the notions of institutional dialogue
and deliberation that catalyze what is a really unusual
constitutional judicial remedy in play here.
The courts don't have this opportunity very often
to speak directly to parliament,
and we expect there's a gear shift
on parliament's part in response.
I'm asking you to think, think about how you might capture
that in your deliberation on a case by case basis,
while also not ignoring
that formally parliament has retained the full power
to make laws under the parliamentary.
So, uh, under the principle of parliamentary sovereignty.
So the, the, the sketch that I've got are things like
consideration of the advice
with an open-minded good faith in a way that engages with,
in detail with the judicial reasoning.
And at least for those first two, that might be able
to be taken as a given.
But I think it's useful in spelling that out.
I suggest the need for reasons
and reasons which are logical, principled,
and generally proportionate to the gravity of the rights
and play, and perhaps reflecting the standard structured
proportionality analysis that is now commonplace in human
rights of pay or by judicial bodies,
attorneys general, and things like that.
But I'm not also wedded to the need
to speak in legal lexicon
because I recognize that
parliament speaks with different voice.
I think importantly, there is a need
to take care about disagreements about the underlying flax
and evidence because you've got the benefit
of a advice from a judicial body on these points.
And I'm suggesting you one possibility is there might be a
need to accept adequate
or receive adequate contrary evidence
and make specific factual findings if you wish to

But I think you need to, uh,
have an evidential foundation from that.
And, um, it may be that that's helped by reports

which has done the heavy lifting there.
So you can, you can draw on
that evidential fa findings if you want
to make contrary recommendations
or findings, uh,
different from the Supreme Court on those factual matters
separate from the evaluative matters.
I should also add, because of this inquiry in the way
that's been constituted, um, I wonder as a matter
of principle, whether you might also want
to routinely seek open
and public submissions on these responses.
My understanding is that these ones have only been done
by invitation only to specific groups.
And I think when we're dealing with a response and,
and, uh, uh, to a declaration in this way, there is a role
for the community at large to be able to speak in
that conversation, which is anticipated.
So, um, uh, they're just some working suggestions.
I'm happy to pick up that
conversation on any of those points.
But, but just to close with the, the basic points
formally there, there are two polemic positions.
Parliament and its committees might adopt
as a posture when responding
to advice from the courts about human rights
through a declaration and consistency.
On the one hand, parliament can quickly double down
and condone the impugned legislation as a matter,
as parliamentary sovereignty allows.
On the other hand, parliament could
or could be expected to fully hate the advice in the name
of protecting human rights.
What I'm saying is declarations inconsistency have developed
as a middle way through those polar positions where
in theory, at least advices offered grappled with
and responded to in a meaningful way, sometimes
with legitimate disagreement.
With that advice. Important, uh, postscript there,
my main suggestion, the C committee should think about
spelling out some working expectations about
how it should work in practice in that middle space.

how to have those conversations.

significance of the declaration.
And then Mr. Macklin, I think we'll spend more time speaking
about the practical points, um, of where to from here, um,
as set out in our written submissions.
Um, the first point I'd like to make is about the importance
of the obligations at stake here.
And it's, the obvious one is obvi, is that
there's domestic human rights legislation
that issue our own Bill of Rights Act,
but the, the legislation sits within an
international law framework as well.
Um, human Rights Commission, uh, their submissions
to the committee touch on that,
those international obligations as well, in particular,
the International Covenant for Civil
and Political Rights that is relevant to the Declaration.
So New Zealand is subject to a, a Universal Periodic review,
uh, before the United Nations Human Rights Council.
And it's highly likely that this declaration
and the parliament's response to it will be relevant
to the Human Rights Council's consideration
of New Zealand's human Rights record.
Um, so it will have an impact, uh, that the response
to it in particular on New Zealand's international
reputation for upholding human rights.
Um, and the only other point I wanted to make,
which I think touches on some questions that I heard, um,
of the, uh, law association just, um,
before we came online, uh,
and that is that the Law society shares the view
of the Law Commission, which I'm sure you heard earlier
today, um, that this
and of the Supreme Court in, in chisel,
that the preventive measures for sexual
and violent offending has an important role to play,
protecting the community from harm,
and the law needs to provide for them.
And the question really is how do we do
that whilst also protecting New Zealand rights, um, sorry,
human rights and New Zealand's international reputation
for protecting human rights, even of, excuse me, our most,
um, often our most vulnerable, um,
members of society in prison.
So the law should continue to provide for them,
but the question is how,
and for that I will pass over to Chris to, um,
continue the Law Society submission.
Yes. Uh, thank you, Monique.
And, and, uh,
the Law Society arrives, um, at this, uh,
particular hearing, standing literally on the shoulders
of giants in terms of the academic analysis
and the very careful work that's gone before us today.
And you've already heard, I'm aware from the Law Commission,
uh, you've heard from, uh, the Law Association
and of course, professor Dean Knight as well.
And rather than sort of rehash that material, what we sought
to focus on in our written submission for you is just a sort
of pragmatic step back and look at the way forward.
We commend to this committee, the Law Commission's report
and conclusions in terms of where to,
but even putting that to one side, we identified two
main pathways that the committee could recommend
for further legislative work.
The first would be to change the sentencing regime
to accommodate preventive measures.
Um, and the second is to find a post sentencing regime,
so respectively called Pathways A and B and our submissions.
Um, we noted that there's a lot of work to do
to flesh out the details on either regime.
We also note and accept the law commission's findings,
that the latter being a post sentence regime change
would be preferable.
Um, so we keep a boot in each camp,
but obviously respect the law commission's view,
and note that there's just a lot of work required
to really flesh out what would be the key details
of whichever pathway that is, that's taken.
Uh, we've supplemented that
with some recommendations about interim,
so addressing issues with retrospective orders
that have been impugned by the chisel analysis, uh, making
or suggesting that, um, we find a way
to have high court review of existing, uh, such orders
and then changes to the,
the relevant acts being the Parole Act
and the, um, PPO Act to ensure
that they are framed explicitly towards least
restrictive outcomes.
Now, um, all of this material,
obviously in the written submission,
which we take is read very mindful of questions, but
before we get there, I just thought, um, it behooves me is,
um, Monique has done too to address some of the ly material
because I've been observing the, the hearing from, from one
and, um, for the criminal law committee
and criminal practitioners in particular, I just wanted to,
to note with the utmost respect, the false dichotomy
that can sneak in when reviewing this material from the
outside about victim versus offender.
Now, obviously, that's not a false dichotomy case to case.
So when one person hurts another,
and these are very serious cases that we're dealing
with here, the victim offender relationship, clear.
But when you take just one step back, the, the sort
of cliche that criminal practitioners have, both prosecutors
and defense counsel, and as a barrister,
I actually do both types of brief, is
that hurt people, hurt people.
And so when we're talking about having a victim's focus,
having a victim lens
and making sure that we protect people,
that doesn't just mean locking up people
and throwing away the key.
It means preventing future harm.
Because the reality is that, you know, subject, even
to Ms Berg's comments earlier, for the vast majority
of prisoners, there is going to be a statutory release date.
They are going to be released.
And if you don't find a way to balance rehabilitative needs
and prospects with protection,
then you're not really protecting people
by just locking away offenders and keeping them inside
and then tiffing them out at the end of a sentence.
And so I speak very loosely there,
but I just wanted to point out
that the idea in a holistic way is to find a balance
where the preventive measures both serve to keep
existing victims safe, but also prevent future victimization
through effective rehabilitation.
And that's where the balance is.
No one in this conversation is talking about
removing preventive measures.
There's academic and organization, consensus,
law Association, law Society, law Commission,
that preventive measures serve a purpose and are needed.
What is required though, is that balance to ensure
that they are effective and justified.
And in the broadest sense, the issues with justification
can be in large parts, come down to the effectiveness
of rehabilitative efforts versus the punitive nature
of ongoing restrictions.
And so the Supreme Court has accepted,
you can impose preventive orders, they do restrict rights,
and that can be justified if,
and it's the, if that really will require quite a lot more
detailed analysis and work,
because they can be justified if
the restrictive measures are imposed
to effectively manage risk and rehabilitate people.
So, uh, I just,
and I'm sort of getting ahead of the questions there,
but I just need, we,
We do have a couple of questions, so thank you Ms.
Macklin. First, uh, question is from, uh, Ms.
Vols, uh, thank you both for the submission.
And, and thank you also, Chris, for those,
your general thoughts as well,
which I think are incredibly important
for us to bear in mind.
Um, I lean towards your option B, um,
but also one of the, the levers
that I think can help in terms of determining reasonableness
or not, uh, procedural measures we can put in place.
So this is the duration of the order itself, the period
of the review, uh, whether it's possible
to initiate a review in between ordinary periods,
and who can do that, whether it's, um,
the individual concern, whether it's a victim,
whether it's corrections.
Um, I noted that I think it was your submission
that set up the three year period for ordinary review,
or maybe it was the law commission,
but I, I wonder if you could just generally comment about
the period of the order, the period of ordinary review,
and any views on initiation of a review
between those standard periods.
Um, I, I can only comment in the broader sense in the
sense that we recommend a process of review.
Um, forgive me, I did review my notes before one,
but I don't recall us proposing a term as such.
And I think the society really is here to say
these mechanisms are important.
Things like review and a proper process are important,
but the details need a lot more work.
Um, and so it wouldn't be for me to stand in
and give you any of those details when I think our main
position is that, that the best work comes ahead of us
in terms of setting up a robust review process.
Um, I, I'd pause to note that it's a, a little,
if we use the model like parole,
and look, I think I can say safely, um, my own view
as opposed to the societies is that pathway B is preferable,
and I know the law commission go that way as well.
Um, I don't think the society
advocates either pathway though.
We just note that those are the only practical ways
to address the declaration.
And so however it's done, if we lean into that sort
of parole model, then it could be that all
of those parties you listed, victim offender, um, court
or parole board itself, could all initiate a review if,
if the regime is geared that way.
Okay. We're gonna have the last question
because we're running outta time,
but, um, we've had plenty of time.
So Dr. Nan actually, uh, sorry.
Chair, I think, I think
Chris has answered that particularly.
All right. Okay, cool. Yeah, really, really well. Right.
Anyone else with another question? I think we're good, Pete.
Look, um, again, everyone, you, you can't hear,
but everyone's acknowledging a great submission.
I want to thank you both for coming on board.
We've had, uh, four very powerful submissions today,
which has been very helpful for our thinking.
So thank you for joining us. Thank you. Thank you both.
Thank you. Very, thank you. Yeah.
Professor, I'm gonna have to ask you to leave.
Um, not 'cause we don't want you, but, um, but thank you.
Thank you for coming.

source_metadata.title: 2026/02/12 - JU
source_metadata.description:
Thursday 12 February 2026 | Justice Committee

Notice of declarations of inconsistency: Public Protection Orders under the Public Safety (Public Protection Orders) Act 2014 and Extended Supervision Orders under the Parole Act 2002

Submitter:

Law Commission

Claudia Geiringer, Commissioner
Thomas Buocz, Senior Legal and Policy Adviser

Dean Knight
The Law Association New Zealand

Marie Dyhrberg KC

New Zealand Law Society

Chris Macklin, Criminal Law Committee convener
Monique van Alphen Fyfe, Human Rights and Privacy Committee member
